channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: White House Warned Staff Not to Engage in Insider Trading Amid War With Iran
item.description: The directive came amid a surge of suspiciously well-timed trades on oil and prediction markets just ahead of crucial moments in the conflict.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Reporters working outside of the West Wing of the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: What to Know About the U.S. Military Draft Pool and Automatic Registration
item.description: For decades, draft-eligible men ages 18 to 25 have been required to register with the Selective Service System. Most states offer a registration option on driver’s license applications.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Office of Information and Regulatory Affairs (US)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: U.S. Army soldiers during combat training in Honolulu, Hawaii, in 2024.

item.title: Trump Attacks Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly Over Iran War Criticism
item.description: In a lengthy social media post, the president attacked Tucker Carlson, Megyn Kelly and others in starkly personal terms. He also criticized the editorial board of The Wall Street Journal.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump took aim at several conservatives in a long and often personal post on Truth Social, his social media platform, on Thursday.

item.title: Intelligence Court Renews Section 702 Surveillance Program
item.description: A classified ruling recertified the Section 702 program for a year but objected to systems that can filter Americans’ messages outside querying limits.
item.dc:creator: Charlie Savage
item.category: Wiretapping and Other Eavesdropping Devices and Methods
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard/Reuters
item.media:description: Kash Patel, the F.B.I. director, attended a classified Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act briefing at the Capitol last month.

item.title: How Trump’s Advisers Felt About Going to War With Iran
item.description: Our reporters Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan discuss how individual members of President Trump’s administration felt in the leadup to the war in Iran, and how they communicated their thoughts to Mr. Trump.
item.dc:creator: Maggie Haberman, Jonathan Swan, Christina Shaman, John Pappas and Ray Whitehouse
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Student Hit by Projectile During ‘No Kings’ Protest Lost an Eye, Lawyer Says
item.description: The student, Tucker Collins, 18, was observing demonstrators in Los Angeles when he was struck, the lawyer said.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Jill Connelly/Associated Press
item.media:description: Federal officers shot a chemical irritant at protesters in Los Angeles during a “No Kings” protest on March 28.

item.title: Amid Trump’s Threats, NATO Labors to Survive the Iran War
item.description: President Trump is citing the unwillingness of European nations to back the United States in the conflict as another reason to scale back or abandon the alliance. And he still wants Greenland.
item.dc:creator: Anton Troianovski
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump and Mark Rutte, the secretary general of NATO, in the Netherlands in June. The two met again in Washington this week, as the war in Iran has deepened the gulf between Mr. Trump and the alliance.

item.title: Strained Postal Service Proposes Price Increases and Delays Retirement Funds
item.description: The moves are the latest attempts to stave off a looming financial crisis, and come on top of an 8 percent package surcharge that will take effect later this month.
item.dc:creator: Adam Sella
item.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
item.media:description: U.S.P.S. regularly contributes about $400 million a month to the Federal Employees Retirement System, which provides retirement funds for Postal Service and other government employees.

item.title: Rodolfo Acuña, 93, Forthright Scholar at Forefront of Chicano Studies, Dies
item.description: An activist in the academy, he wrote a foundational text in the field, “Occupied America: A History of Chicanos.” It is still in print and still assigned to students.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.category: Occupied America: A History of Chicanos (Book)

item.title: Florida Attorney General Investigates OpenAI and ChatGPT Over F.S.U. Shooting
item.description: The state’s attorney general, James Uthmeier, said ChatGPT “may likely have been used to assist” the suspect in last year’s shooting at Florida State University.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Erich Martin for The New York Times
item.media:description: Students and members of the community leaving flowers outside the taped-off perimeter on Florida State University’s campus after a shooting that left two people dead.

item.title: Israel Complicates Trump’s Push for Peace With Iran
item.description: President Trump said he had asked Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu to scale back Israel’s military campaign in Lebanon.
item.dc:creator: Michael Crowley
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Trump and Mr. Netanyahu jointly launched war on Iran six weeks ago.

item.title: Top Regent Defends Firing of Wisconsin University Leader
item.description: In testimony before state lawmakers, regents suggested that Jay O. Rothman had been well aware of the board’s concerns about his leadership.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: John Hart/Wisconsin State Journal, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Jay O. Rothman, center, was the president of the Universities of Wisconsin since 2022.

item.title: Victim in ICE Shooting Suffered at Least Six Wounds, His Lawyer Says
item.description: Agents shot Carlos Ivan Mendoza Hernandez in Patterson, Calif., this week. His lawyer said Thursday that they had fired at Mr. Hernandez before he tried to flee.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Andy Alfaro/Modesto Bee, via ZUMA Press Wire
item.media:description: The scene of the shooting was quickly filled with law enforcement agents.

item.title: Melania Trump Says She Was Not Associated With Jeffrey Epstein
item.description: Responding to what she said were smears, the first lady said she never had knowledge of Jeffrey Epstein’s abuse and was not a victim of his. She called for a congressional hearing for his victims.
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh
item.media:credit: Evan Vucci/Reuters
item.media:description: Melania Trump, the first lady, made an unexpected statement about Jeffrey Epstein at the White House on Thursday.

item.title: Questions About N.C. Stabbing Suspect’s Mental Illness Loom Over Case
item.description: Decarlos Brown Jr., who is charged with murdering a Ukrainian immigrant, was found incapable of proceeding to state trial. A federal case against him will come first.
item.dc:creator: Eduardo Medina
item.media:credit: Travis Dove for The New York Times
item.media:description: A vigil for Iryna Zarutska, who was killed at a light rail station in Charlotte.

item.title: In Private Meeting, Trump Soothes Disenchanted MAHA Leaders
item.description: White House officials solicited messaging ideas from leaders of the Make America Healthy Again movement, which has soured on some recent administration actions.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:credit: Al Drago for The New York Times
item.media:description: Many of the MAHA Moms are former Democrats or independents who took a leap of faith in supporting President Trump in 2024 after Robert F. Kennedy Jr. endorsed him.

item.title: 5 Takeaways From the U.S. Push Against the Far Left
item.description: The Trump administration has made countering antifa and other far-left groups a counterterrorism priority, despite increasing threats from the Middle East.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Martin Lelievre/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Antifa protesters at a rally in Paris last month.

item.title: Trump Officials Push Allies to Pursue Antifa and Far Left as Terrorist Threats
item.description: The Trump administration aims to deploy counterterrorism tools against far-left groups, even as it has offered little evidence they present a dire threat.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas, Alan Feuer, Matina Stevis-Gridneff, Edward Wong and Jim Tankersley
item.media:credit: Elisa Marchina/NurPhoto, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Left-wing protesters clashing with police in Milan in February. A State Department official said the episode showed why Western governments must combat “antifa and far-left terrorism.”

item.title: David B. Cornstein, Envoy Who Built U.S. Ties to Orban, Dies at 87
item.description: A former jewelry-counter magnate, he served in Hungary under his friend President Trump, strengthening relations between the two countries as Orban tilted rightward.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Jim McKnight/Associated Press
item.media:description: David Cornstein in 2001, when he was active in Republican politics in New York. Later, as ambassador to Hungary under President Trump, he joined in abandoning the Obama administration’s hard-line approach to Mr. Orban’s illiberal governance and tried flattery instead.

item.title: Supreme Court Secrecy Includes Reasons for Recusal
item.description: Justices often don’t disclose why they disqualify themselves from hearing cases. Their silence echoes the court’s unexplained emergency orders.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Richard Lazarus in his office in Cambridge, Mass.

item.title: Brown University Keeps Police Chief Who Took Over After Shooting
item.description: Hugh T. Clements Jr. was a fixture of the Providence Police Department before becoming Brown’s interim chief after the fatal attack in December.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: Glenn Osmundson/USA Today Network, via Reuters
item.media:description: Former Providence Police Chief Hugh T. Clements Jr. was chosen to oversee campus security at Brown University.

item.title: San Quentin Uses Sports as Rehabilitation. It Could Soon Be a Model Elsewhere.
item.description: Eli Tan, who covered the San Quentin Giants last year, shares how the story inspired one effort to expand prison sports in California.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Brian L. Frank for The New York Times
item.media:description: California lawmakers introduced a bill last month that would help expand athletic programming across state prisons.

item.title: F.B.I. Arrests Ex-Fort Bragg Employee Accused of Leaking Classified Information to Journalist
item.description: Courtney Williams, who worked at Fort Bragg in North Carolina, was accused of leaking classified information to a reporter.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Fort Bragg in 2025. Courtney Williams worked in a support role there from 2010 to 2016.

item.title: At Least One Dead After Parking Garage Partially Collapses in Philadelphia
item.description: Two others are missing after a roof segment fell in a building under construction on Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Victor Mather
item.media:credit: WPVI-TV/6ABC, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The parking garage that partially collapsed in the Grays Ferry section of Philadelphia.

item.title: The First Race of the L.A. Olympics: Buying Tickets
item.description: The winners of a lottery for a presale prioritizing locals often came away with sticker shock. Still, organizers said early sales had “significantly exceeded” those of other Games.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan and Nicole Stock
item.category: Los Angeles Organizing Committee for the Olympic and Paralympic Games 2028 (LA28)
item.media:credit: Damian Dovarganes/Associated Press
item.media:description: Los Angeles Olympic organizers emphasized that a million tickets would be available for $28 each.

item.title: These Homesteaders Prep for the End of the World at Oklahoma Expo
item.description: Homesteading, for all its bucolic imagery, taps into the desire to escape from the disquiet of modern America, where anything can happen.
item.dc:creator: Anemona Hartocollis and Nick Oxford
item.media:credit: Nick Oxford for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kevin Pritchett, one of the founders of the Okie Homesteading Expo, at his homestead in Salina, Okla.

item.title: In a Deep Red Town In Pennsylvania, Locals Vent Over a Planned ICE Detention Center
item.description: The Tremont, Pa., area has roughly 2,000 residents and limited resources. The Trump administration plans to convert a warehouse there to hold nearly four times as many people.
item.dc:creator: Chris Hippensteel
item.media:credit: Rachel Wisniewski for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Department of Homeland Security plans to turn a warehouse in Tremont, Pa., into an immigrant detention center, but many local residents complain that they have been given little input.

item.title: How Trump Purged Immigration Judges to Speed Up Deportations
item.description: Judges are ordering an unprecedented number of people deported after coming under significant pressure from the administration to do so or risk losing their jobs.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Nehamas, Allison McCann, Steven Rich, Jazmine Ulloa and Hamed Aleaziz

item.title: Why Manatees Need Humans to Slow Down and Pay Attention
item.description: These gentle giants forage in shallow waters, primarily along the coast of Florida, and often have fatal encounters with boats. What can be done to help them?
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.category: Florida Fish and Wildlife Conservation Commission
item.media:credit: Zack Wittman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Manatees in the Wakulla River in Florida in 2024. Collisions with boats accounted for 25 percent of their deaths in Florida last year, state figures show.

item.title: On Social Media, Democratic Politicians Are Letting the F-Word Fly
item.description: Democratic politicians are swearing with glee. It is usually aimed at President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Kellen Browning and Matt Zdun
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: “I’m a pretty nice guy, but I swear,” said Representative Robert Garcia, Democrat of California.
